food_subgroup: Noodle dishes with meat and dumplings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for char siew wonton noodle. FOODON_03315595 (noodle dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for wonton noodle with char siew in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for char siew wonton noodle or sufficiently close Cantonese noodle dish. Null is appropriate.

allergens: gluten (wheat flour in noodles and wonton wrappers)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of char siew wonton noodle is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of cooked egg noodles (roughly 150 g), two to three wontons (approximately 60–80 g), several slices of char siew (approximately 80–100 g), and a small amount of sauce and garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Wonton Noodles (Dry-tossed style, with char siew and wontons) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Health Promotion Board Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists wonton noodle (dry-tossed) with char siew as a composite dish. Values are adapted to per-100 g basis from the HPB entry for 'Wonton Noodle, dry' (approximately 350 g serving). The caramel glaze on the char siew adds marginal sugar; this is reflected in the sugar_g estimate. Where HPB does not report a micronutrient, the value is set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of egg noodles and composite noodle dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for char siew wonton noodle as a composite dish. The GI of 47 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for egg noodles (GI approximately 40–54, mean ~47, tested in healthy subjects). The protein and fat content of the char siew and wontons further attenuate the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (47 × 74.9 g available carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 35.2, rounded to 35.3. gi_category set to 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per serving) from soy sauce, seasoning, and wonton broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium soy sauce and avoid adding extra seasoning, as a single serving can contribute approximately 70–84% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~35 per serving) from refined egg noodles, partially offset by protein and fat from char siew and wontons
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (approximately 200–250 g) and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate the postprandial glucose response, given the moderate-to-high glycemic load of a full serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from char siew (roast pork) and lard, combined with high sodium from soy-based sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption due to the combined burden of saturated fat (~1.9 g per 100 g, ~6.7 g per serving) and high sodium, and should request leaner char siew cuts and reduced sauce where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Char Siew Wonton Noodle (叉烧云吞面) is a classic Cantonese noodle dish widely popular in Singapore, Hong Kong, and among Chinese diaspora communities across Southeast Asia. The dish consists of springy, thin egg noodles — typically cooked al dente and tossed in a savoury sauce made from soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and lard — served alongside slices of char siew (Chinese BBQ roast pork glazed with honey or maltose for a caramelised, slightly sweet crust) and handmade wontons filled with seasoned minced pork and/or prawns. The noodles have a firm, chewy texture with a rich umami flavour from the sauce, while the char siew contributes sweetness and smokiness, and the wontons add a delicate, silky bite. In Singapore, the dish is commonly served dry-tossed (kon lo) with a small bowl of wonton soup on the side, or in soup form. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 588 kcal, 28.7 g protein, 20.3 g fat, and 74.9 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1680 mg per serving) due to soy-based sauces, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 47 (low GI), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate-to-high (~35), relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish contains gluten (wheat noodles and wonton wrappers), eggs, soy, and potentially shellfish allergens.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for dry-tossed wonton noodle with char siew, which is the closest approved primary source for this Singaporean dish. The GI is an analogous estimate based on egg noodle GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of the composite dish as served (noodles, char siew, wontons, and sauce combined). Serving size of 350 g is an estimate based on typical Singapore hawker portion sizes. 2. The 'caramel' descriptor in the input food name refers to the honey-caramel glaze on the char siew; this is a standard preparation variant and does not materially alter the nutritional profile relative to standard char siew wonton noodle. 3. Sentinel values (-1) are used for vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg because these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish. 4. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate from egg noodle data (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for char siew wonton noodle as a composite dish was identified in the approved sources. GL per serving = (47 × 74.9) / 100 = 35.2, reported as 35.3. 5. Halal status is marked false as char siew traditionally uses pork and is not halal-certified unless specifically stated by the restaurant. 6. The input cuisine_tags included 'Roasted Duck' and 'Duck Dish', which appear to be erroneous tags from the source restaurant (Hennessy Duck - Funan) and are not reflected in this record, which correctly identifies the dish as a char siew (pork) wonton noodle.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_name from promotional input 'Caramel Char Siew Wanton Noodle 蜜汁叉烧云吞面' to canonical English 'Char Siew Wonton Noodle'.
Added all mandatory Singaporean multilingual local names: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Sourced nutrient values from HPB Singapore Foodo (approved primary source) and adapted to per-100 g basis.
Estimated GI from University of Sydney GI Database egg noodle data with full documentation of analogous estimation basis.
Populated all three required health context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Wrote natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters with full clinical and cultural context.
Ensured all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes with explicit source justification.
Removed erroneous 'Roasted Duck' and 'Duck Dish' cuisine tags from the input that were artifacts of the source restaurant.
Set gi_category to 'low' correctly per ≤55 threshold rule (GI=47).
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: 'closest_parent' and 'none'.
